titleStmt.title: Josef [Joseph] Knettel
born Düsseldorf, August 28, 1875; died Bingen, December 5, 1972

publicationStmt.publisher: Schenker Documents Online in association with the Centre for Computing in the Humanities

sourceDesc.p: No source: created in electronic format.

encodingDesc.p: Encoding according to the CCH TEI Guidelines

div.p: German conductor and composer based largely in Bingen am Rhein; friend of Reinhard Oppel in the late 1920s and early 1930s.

div.head: Career Summary
div.p: Josef Knettel studied composition with Arnold Mendelssohn in Frankfurt, where he also studied conducting. He moved to Bingen am Rhein in 1902 and for more than half a century was conductor and music director of musical groups, including the St. Caecilia Society, the Men’s Singing Society, the Orchestral Union, the Evangelical Church Choir, and the Bad Kreuznach Concert Society. Although he was not Jewish, Knettel served as organist of the Reform Synagogue in Bingen until 1938.

His many compositions include the vocal works Der Erfolg (The Success) and Altes Tanslied (Tanzen und Springen) (Old Dance-song: Dancing and Jumping). Manuscript and printed scores by Knettel and concert programs are now preserved in the Joseph Knettel Memorial Collection at the University of North Texas Library, Denton. Annemarie Maas-Knettel’s short biography was published as: Josef Knettel: Aus dem Leben des Künstlers (Bad Kreuznach: Friedrich Fiedler, 1977).

div.head: Knettel and Oppel
div.p: Knettel and Oppel must first have met as students of Arnold Mendelssohn at the Frankfurt-am-Main Conservatory, and remained close friends thereafter. It was Knettel who performed Oppel’s a capella Mass in Kreuznach and Bingen in February 1927; in 1933 Oppel commented on compositions by Knettel at the latter’s request; Oppel also sent a photograph of Knettel to Schenker in 1933, and Knettel wrote several hymns in praise of Germany that he sent to Oppel, one of which was forwarded to Schenker (OJ 13/10, [40], [41], [176], [181], [185], [189]. Extensive correspondence between the two men is preserved in the University of North Texas Library. Knettel and Schenker are not known to have corresponded directly.
